rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the source via zero-waste/single-use reduction. Quotes: policy_statement: "stop serving food/drink prepared in single-use catering equipment" and "only mains water" as part of a "zero-waste strategy"; verbatim_text: "will no longer serve food and drink prepared in single-use catering equipment, and will only serve mains water". This matches the canonical mechanism (single-use reduction -> waste reduction) and fits the assigned primary_category Resource Efficiency despite the gate missing an outcome token.
policy_statement: From July 2023, stop serving food/drink prepared in single-use catering equipment and serve only mains water for City Council activities and City-organised events (zero-waste strategy).
mechanism_description: The City Council will stop serving food and drink prepared in single-use catering equipment and will only serve mains water at its activities and organised events, reducing single-use items and the waste they create.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (food/drink provision) → ↓ material throughput (single-use items) → ↓ upstream manufacturing + ↓ waste management → ↓ energy use and GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Public-sector procurement and event operations shift from single-use catering items and bottled/packaged water toward reusables and mains water service.  
2) Mediating physical/industrial process: Lower demand for virgin plastic and single-use products reduces petrochemical feedstock extraction/refining, polymer production, product manufacturing, and transport; reduced discarded plastics also reduces collection, sorting, incineration/landfill handling and associated fuel use.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in energy consumption and associated CO₂e emissions across the product life cycle (materials production dominates in many cases), with additional reductions from avoided waste treatment emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided life-cycle GHG emissions by reducing production and disposal of single-use plastic items.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Reducing single-use plastics reduces material throughput for the same service, which reduces energy use and GHG emissions from manufacturing and waste management.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there is a mitigation outcome (lower CO₂e), the mechanism’s defining lever is waste/material prevention (efficiency of resource use) rather than directly decarbonizing an energy supply or adapting to hazards; thus Resource Efficiency best matches the dominant lever.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy frames the change mainly as litter reduction or marine ecosystem protection, climate benefits may be secondary; if waste is predominantly landfilled with low methane relevance for plastics, benefits still largely come from avoided production energy rather than landfill methane.  
4) Primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism—reducing single-use plastics to reduce solid waste generation—operates through reduced material production/throughput and hence fits Resource Efficiency as the shared primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy is best tagged as procurement reform, event management, packaging regulation, or water infrastructure is policy-specific and requires policy-level evidence beyond the mechanism label.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implementation replaces single-use plastics with higher-carbon single-use alternatives (e.g., heavy single-use glass/metal) or drives substantial additional washing energy for reusables with carbon-intensive electricity, net mitigation could be reduced; nevertheless the mechanism remains material/waste prevention (Resource Efficiency) as the primary classification.
key_indicators: "will no longer serve food and drink prepared in single-use catering equipment"; "will only serve mains water"; "from July 2023"; "as part of its own activities and at events it organises"
co_benefits: Reduced litter and local pollution; potential cost savings from avoiding single-use purchases; reduced bottled-water transport impacts
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a procurement requirement for catering vendors, but the wording is framed as an operational prohibition/requirement for municipal activities and events, so classified as a regulatory mandate.
